dependencies.description: The exporter must have IP reachability to the collector via the configured source interface.  If the collector is in a different VRF (e.g., management), the export must specify the correct VRF context.
dependencies.name: IP reachability to collector

dependencies.description: NetFlow/IPFIX export uses UDP (commonly port 2055, 9996, or 9995).  UDP is unreliable — dropped export packets are not retransmitted.  ACLs and firewalls must permit the export port.
dependencies.name: UDP transport

failure_modes.description: Flow data never arrives at the collector.  The exporter is caching and aging flows normally, but exported UDP packets are lost in transit or never sent.  This is the most common NetFlow issue and is almost always a reachability or configuration problem between the exporter and the collector.

resolutions.action: Verify collector IP and port match the collector application configuration
Verify collector IP and port match the collector application configuration
Verify collector IP and port match the collector application configuration
Ensure the source interface used for export has IP reachability to the collector: ping from source interface
Ensure the source interface used for export has IP reachability to the collector: ping from source interface
Ensure the source interface used for export has IP reachability to the collector: ping from source interface
If collector is in management VRF, add 'use-vrf management' (NX-OS) or set source interface in the correct VRF
If collector is in management VRF, add 'use-vrf management' (NX-OS) or set source interface in the correct VRF
If collector is in management VRF, add 'use-vrf management' (NX-OS) or set source interface in the correct VRF
Check ACLs on all in-path devices for the export UDP port
Check ACLs on all in-path devices for the export UDP port
Check ACLs on all in-path devices for the export UDP port
Reduce export MTU or enable fragmentation if needed
Reduce export MTU or enable fragmentation if needed
Reduce export MTU or enable fragmentation if needed

root_causes.cause: Wrong collector IP address or UDP port configured on the exporter
Source interface for export is unreachable from the collector (routing or ACL)
ACL on the exporter or in-path device blocking UDP export traffic (port 2055/9996/9995)
VRF mismatch — export configured in global table but collector reachable only via management VRF (or vice versa)
Collector firewall blocking inbound UDP on the export port
MTU issue — large export packets fragmented and dropped in transit

symptoms: Collector shows no data or gaps in flow records
show flow exporter statistics shows incrementing 'packets sent' but collector receives nothing
show flow exporter statistics shows 'packets dropped' or 'no FIB' errors
SNMP-based bandwidth graphs exist but flow-based visibility is missing

verify_commands.confirms_if: Packets sent incrementing with zero drops confirms export is leaving the device
verify_commands.look_for: Check 'packets sent' vs 'packets dropped'.  Look for 'no FIB' or 'adjacency failure' which indicate routing problems to the collector.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Packets sent incrementing with zero drops confirms export is leaving the device
verify_commands.look_for: Check 'packets sent' vs 'packets dropped'.  Look for 'no FIB' or 'adjacency failure' which indicate routing problems to the collector.

verify_commands.look_for: Check 'packets sent' vs 'packets dropped'.  Look for 'no FIB' or 'adjacency failure' which indicate routing problems to the collector.

verify_commands.look_for: Verify the monitor is in 'active' status and associated with the correct exporter and record.

verify_commands.look_for: Check destination IP, port, source interface, and VRF.  Verify 'flows exported' counter is incrementing.

failure_modes.description: Flow cache operations consume excessive CPU.  On platforms without hardware NetFlow support, all flow classification and cache operations happen in software, which can overwhelm the control plane on high-speed interfaces.  Even on hardware-capable platforms, misconfigured timeouts or missing sampling can cause high CPU from excessive cache management and export processing.

resolutions.action: Enable sampling (1:100 to 1:1000) on high-speed interfaces to reduce flow creation rate
Enable sampling (1:100 to 1:1000) on high-speed interfaces to reduce flow creation rate
Enable sampling (1:100 to 1:1000) on high-speed interfaces to reduce flow creation rate
Increase inactive timeout to reduce cache churn (30-60s instead of 15s)
Increase inactive timeout to reduce cache churn (30-60s instead of 15s)
Increase inactive timeout to reduce cache churn (30-60s instead of 15s)
Verify platform supports hardware-based NetFlow/FNF — check line card capabilities
Verify platform supports hardware-based NetFlow/FNF — check line card capabilities
Verify platform supports hardware-based NetFlow/FNF — check line card capabilities
Reduce the number of flow record fields to simplify cache lookup
Reduce the number of flow record fields to simplify cache lookup
Reduce the number of flow record fields to simplify cache lookup
Consider applying flow monitor only on ingress OR egress, not both, if CPU is constrained
Consider applying flow monitor only on ingress OR egress, not both, if CPU is constrained
Consider applying flow monitor only on ingress OR egress, not both, if CPU is constrained

root_causes.cause: Too many active flows in the cache (millions of micro-flows from scanning/DDoS)
Aggressive active/inactive timeouts causing frequent cache churn
Sampling not enabled on high-speed interfaces (10G/40G/100G) — every packet processed
Hardware NetFlow not available on the platform or line card, falling back to software
Multiple flow monitors applied to the same interface (ingress + egress) doubling the load

symptoms: High CPU utilization from FLOW-CACHE, FNF, or NetFlow process
Slow CLI response on the device
Control-plane protocols (BGP, OSPF) affected by CPU starvation
'show processes cpu' shows flow-related process at top

verify_commands.confirms_if: Flow process at top of CPU consumers confirms NetFlow is the cause
verify_commands.look_for: Identify which flow-related process is consuming CPU.  Look for FLOW-CACHE, FNF, or NetFlow Export.

verify_commands.look_for: Identify which flow-related process is consuming CPU.  Look for FLOW-CACHE, FNF, or NetFlow Export.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Active entries near cache size confirms cache overload
verify_commands.look_for: Check 'active entries' vs 'cache size'.  If active entries are near the cache limit, the cache is overloaded.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Active entries near cache size confirms cache overload
verify_commands.look_for: Check 'active entries' vs 'cache size'.  If active entries are near the cache limit, the cache is overloaded.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Active entries near cache size confirms cache overload
verify_commands.look_for: Check 'active entries' vs 'cache size'.  If active entries are near the cache limit, the cache is overloaded.

failure_modes.description: The flow cache is full and new flows cannot be created, causing flow records to be dropped.  This typically happens during DDoS attacks or network scans that create millions of unique micro-flows (unique src/dst IP + port combinations) that exhaust the cache before inactive timeout can expire them.

resolutions.action: Increase flow cache size: 'cache entries <N>' under the flow monitor
Increase flow cache size: 'cache entries <N>' under the flow monitor
Increase flow cache size: 'cache entries <N>' under the flow monitor
Enable sampling to reduce flow creation rate during traffic spikes
Enable sampling to reduce flow creation rate during traffic spikes
Enable sampling to reduce flow creation rate during traffic spikes
Reduce inactive timeout to expire stale flows faster (10-15s)
Reduce inactive timeout to expire stale flows faster (10-15s)
Reduce inactive timeout to expire stale flows faster (10-15s)
Use flow record with fewer match fields to reduce flow cardinality (e.g., drop L4 ports)
Use flow record with fewer match fields to reduce flow cardinality (e.g., drop L4 ports)
Use flow record with fewer match fields to reduce flow cardinality (e.g., drop L4 ports)
Consider permanent cache mode for top-talker analysis instead of full flow export
Consider permanent cache mode for top-talker analysis instead of full flow export
Consider permanent cache mode for top-talker analysis instead of full flow export

root_causes.cause: Cache size too small for the traffic volume and flow cardinality
DDoS or network scan creating millions of micro-flows that exhaust cache
No sampling configured on high-rate interfaces — every packet creates a flow entry
Inactive timeout too long — stale entries not being expired fast enough

failure_modes.scenario: NetFlow cache overflow — flow cache full, flows dropped
symptoms: 'cache full' or 'flow not created' syslog messages
Incomplete flow data — collector shows gaps or missing traffic
Flow export volume drops despite traffic volume increasing
show flow monitor statistics shows 'flows not added' counter incrementing

verify_commands.confirms_if: Active entries at cache limit with non-zero 'flows not added' confirms overflow
verify_commands.look_for: Compare 'active entries' to 'cache size'.  If they are equal or near-equal, the cache is full.  Check 'flows not added' for drop count.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Active entries at cache limit with non-zero 'flows not added' confirms overflow
verify_commands.look_for: Compare 'active entries' to 'cache size'.  If they are equal or near-equal, the cache is full.  Check 'flows not added' for drop count.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Active entries at cache limit with non-zero 'flows not added' confirms overflow
verify_commands.look_for: Compare 'active entries' to 'cache size'.  If they are equal or near-equal, the cache is full.  Check 'flows not added' for drop count.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Incrementing overflow counters confirm cache exhaustion
verify_commands.look_for: Look for 'flows not added' or 'cache overflows' counters.  These directly indicate dropped flow records.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Incrementing overflow counters confirm cache exhaustion
verify_commands.look_for: Look for 'flows not added' or 'cache overflows' counters.  These directly indicate dropped flow records.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Incrementing overflow counters confirm cache exhaustion
verify_commands.look_for: Look for 'flows not added' or 'cache overflows' counters.  These directly indicate dropped flow records.

failure_modes.description: Flow volumes reported by the collector do not match actual interface counters.  This is usually a sampling misconfiguration where the collector is not properly compensating for the sampling rate, or sampling is applied inconsistently across interfaces.

resolutions.action: Verify sampling rate on all monitored interfaces: 'show sampler'
Verify sampling rate on all monitored interfaces: 'show sampler'
Verify sampling rate on all monitored interfaces: 'show sampler'
Ensure collector knows the sampling rate and multiplies flow counters accordingly
Ensure collector knows the sampling rate and multiplies flow counters accordingly
Ensure collector knows the sampling rate and multiplies flow counters accordingly
Apply consistent sampling rate across all monitored interfaces
Apply consistent sampling rate across all monitored interfaces
Apply consistent sampling rate across all monitored interfaces
Use 1:100 to 1:500 for reasonable accuracy; 1:1000+ only for very high-speed links
Use 1:100 to 1:500 for reasonable accuracy; 1:1000+ only for very high-speed links
Use 1:100 to 1:500 for reasonable accuracy; 1:1000+ only for very high-speed links
Apply sampler to both ingress and egress if bidirectional visibility is required
Apply sampler to both ingress and egress if bidirectional visibility is required
Apply sampler to both ingress and egress if bidirectional visibility is required

root_causes.cause: Sampling rate too aggressive (1:1000+) causing significant statistical error for low-volume flows
Collector not configured with the correct sampling rate multiplier
Asymmetric sampling — sampler applied to ingress but not egress (or different rates on each)
Sampler not applied to all relevant interfaces — some interfaces missing flow data entirely
Random vs deterministic sampling producing different accuracy profiles

failure_modes.scenario: NetFlow sampling rate inaccurate — collected data doesn't match actual traffic
symptoms: Flow-reported traffic volumes are orders of magnitude lower than SNMP interface counters
Some interfaces show flow data while others do not
Asymmetric traffic visibility — inbound flows present but outbound missing (or vice versa)
Collector reports wildly inaccurate bandwidth utilization

purpose: Export IP flow records (source/destination IP, ports, protocol, byte/packet counters) to a collector for traffic analysis, capacity planning, security monitoring, and billing

timers.description: Exports long-lived flows periodically even while they remain active.  When the timer fires, the current counters are exported and the flow entry is reset (not removed) so a new record begins accumulating.
timers.name: Active flow timeout

timers.description: Expires idle flows — if no packets match a cached flow entry for this duration, the entry is exported and removed from the cache, freeing memory.
timers.name: Inactive flow timeout

timers.description: NetFlow v9 and IPFIX use template-based export.  The exporter re-sends template records at this interval so collectors can decode data records.  If the collector misses a template, it cannot parse any data records that reference it.
timers.name: Template refresh interval

config_examples:
flow record MY-RECORD
 match ipv4 source address
 match ipv4 destination address
 match transport source-port
 match transport destination-port
 match ip protocol
 collect counter bytes long
 collect counter packets long
flow exporter MY-EXPORTER
 destination 10.1.1.100
 transport udp 2055
 source Loopback0
 export-protocol netflow-v9
flow monitor MY-MONITOR
 record MY-RECORD
 exporter MY-EXPORTER
 cache timeout active 60
 cache timeout inactive 15
interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1
 ip flow monitor MY-MONITOR input
 ip flow monitor MY-MONITOR output

notes.note: Flexible NetFlow (FNF). 'flow record' + 'flow exporter' + 'flow monitor' + 'ip flow monitor <name> input/output' on interface.  ETA (Encrypted Traffic Analytics) extends NetFlow with TLS metadata for threat visibility in encrypted traffic.  Performance Monitor for ART (Application Response Time) metrics uses the same FNF infrastructure.

config_examples:
sampler-map MY-SAMPLER
 random 1 out-of 100
flow exporter-map MY-EXPORTER
 destination 10.1.1.100
 transport udp 2055
 source Loopback0
 version v9
flow monitor-map MY-MONITOR
 record ipv4
 exporter MY-EXPORTER
 cache timeout active 60
 cache timeout inactive 15
interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/0
 flow ipv4 monitor MY-MONITOR sampler MY-SAMPLER ingress

notes.note: 'flow exporter-map' + 'flow monitor-map' + interface application under 'interface' config.  Supports NetFlow v9 and IPFIX.  'sampler-map' for sampling configuration.  Hardware-based on NCS 5500 and Cisco 8000 — flow classification done in the forwarding ASIC.

config_examples:
feature netflow
flow record MY-RECORD
  match ipv4 source address
  match ipv4 destination address
  match transport source-port
  match transport destination-port
  match ip protocol
  collect counter bytes long
  collect counter packets long
flow exporter MY-EXPORTER
  destination 10.1.1.100 use-vrf management
  transport udp 2055
  source mgmt0
  version 9
flow monitor MY-MONITOR
  record MY-RECORD
  exporter MY-EXPORTER
interface Ethernet1/1
  ip flow monitor MY-MONITOR input

notes.note: Flexible NetFlow (FNF). 'feature netflow'. Flow record + flow exporter + flow monitor + apply to interface. 'flow export destination <ip> <port> use-vrf management'. Hardware-based on Nexus 9000 — no CPU impact for flow classification.  NX-OS uses 'flow record' to define match/collect fields, 'flow exporter' for collector destination, and 'flow monitor' to bind them together.

config_examples:
config sflow enable
config sflow collector add COLLECTOR1 10.1.1.100 --port 6343
config sflow interface enable Ethernet0
config sflow polling-interval 20
config sflow agent-id add Loopback0

notes.note: sFlow (not NetFlow). 'config sflow enable' to activate.  sFlow agent runs in the host OS.  CONFIG_DB SFLOW and SFLOW_COLLECTOR tables store configuration.  Counter and flow sampling via psample kernel module.  sFlow uses a different packet format (RFC 3176) but serves the same traffic visibility purpose.
